# TPC Repair Billing — Time-Series Forecast

**Forecasting monthly average container repair bills using 8 benchmarked models on 1.5M+ real eMARS TPC transaction records.**

![Dashboard Screenshot](forecast_plot.png) 
*12-month forward forecast of average monthly repair bill amount, generated by the winning model.*

## Project Overview

- Built a clean monthly time-series from 1.5M+ repair line items (2015–2018)
- Converted all currencies to USD using transaction-specific exchange rates
- Benchmarked **8 models** spanning classical time-series and machine learning approaches: RandomForest, HistGradientBoosting, SARIMA, Prophet, ETS, and others
- Selected the winning model based on MAE / MAPE on a held-out test period
- Produced a 12-month forward forecast of average monthly repair bill

## Key Skills Demonstrated

- Time-series feature engineering & lag creation
- Machine learning (scikit-learn) vs. classical forecasting methods, benchmarked head-to-head
- Model evaluation and leaderboard comparison across error metrics
- Data cleaning at scale (large CSV handling, currency normalization)
- Python (pandas, matplotlib, scikit-learn, statsmodels)

## Results

- **Best model: RandomForest**, with **MAPE = 3.66%** on the hold-out set — the lowest error among all 8 models tested
- The 12-month forecast shows a stable/flat trend in average monthly repair bill amount
- Recursive forecasting revealed the limitations of tree-based models for extrapolating trends beyond the training range (see below)

**[View Full Notebook →](https://github.com/MChu2019/tpc-repair-forecast/blob/main/Repair_Cost_Recovery_Forecast.ipynb)**

## What I Learned

- Tree-based models (like RandomForest) can't extrapolate trends beyond their training data — they predict within the range they've seen, which matters when forecasting into genuinely new territory
- Proper train/test splitting in time series requires respecting chronological order, not random shuffling
- Comparing multiple forecasting paradigms (ML vs. classical statistical methods) on the same dataset surfaces trade-offs that benchmarking a single model type would miss

## Run Locally

```bash
pip install -r requirements.txt
jupyter notebook Repair_Cost_Recovery_Forecast.ipynb
```

## Files

- `Repair_Cost_Recovery_Forecast.ipynb` — full analysis, model benchmarking, and forecast
- `forecast_plot.png` — final forecast visualization
- `requirements.txt` — dependencies
